content.problemStatement:
Chef has a set SSS containing NNN **distinct** integers.

Chef wants to gift Chefina an array AAA of any finite length such that the following conditions hold true:


Ai∈SA_i \in SAi​∈S ∀i\forall i∀i. In other words, each element of the array AAA should belong to the set SSS.
Mean value of all the elements in AAA is **exactly** XXX.
Find whether there exists an array AAA of finite length satisfying the above conditions.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
First line of each test case contains two integers NNN and XXX - denoting the size of set SSS and the mean value of the required array.
Second line contains NNN distinct integers S1,S2,…,SNS_1, S_2, \dots , S_NS1​,S2​,…,SN​ - denoting the set SSS.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line, `YES` if there exists an array satisfying the given conditions, `NO` otherwise.


You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings `YES`, `yEs`, `yes`, and `yeS` will all be treated as identical).
constraints: 1 <= N <= 10^5 (sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and X — the size of the set S and the required mean value. The second line contains N distinct integers S1, S2, …, SN — the elements of the set.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if an array of finite length with elements from S can have mean exactly X, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: min=1, max=3, X=2 lies between them → YES.
Test case 2: only element is 3, X=5 not equal → NO.
Test case 3: min=4, max=6, X=5 between → YES.
Test case 4: single element 5 equals X → YES.
First case: min=5, max=10, X=4 < min → NO. Second case: min=2, max=12, X=7 between → YES.
X equals the minimum element, so YES.

solution.approach: For any array whose elements are taken from S, its average must lie between the smallest and the largest element of S (inclusive). Conversely, if X lies between min(S) and max(S), we can always construct an array: pick two numbers a ≤ X ≤ b from S and use (b‑X) copies of a and (X‑a) copies of b; the resulting average is X. Therefore the answer is YES iff min(S) ≤ X ≤ max(S). The algorithm for each test case is: read N and X, read the N numbers while tracking the minimum and maximum, then check the condition and output the result.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the size of the set. Overall O(total N) ≤ O(2·10^5).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides input storage.
